questions.question: Match each optimizer to its key characteristic.
categories: Uses a static learning rate and only the current gradient
Accumulates past gradients into a velocity to smooth updates
Adapts learning rate per parameter but it keeps shrinking
Combines momentum with adaptive per-parameter learning rates

correct_mapping.Vanilla SGD: Uses a static learning rate and only the current gradient
correct_mapping.SGD with Momentum: Accumulates past gradients into a velocity to smooth updates
correct_mapping.AdaGrad: Adapts learning rate per parameter but it keeps shrinking
correct_mapping.Adam: Combines momentum with adaptive per-parameter learning rates

questions.explanation: Vanilla SGD applies a fixed learning rate to the current gradient each step. Momentum adds a velocity term from past gradients to smooth the path. AdaGrad adapts per-parameter rates but suffers from ever-decreasing learning rates. Adam combines the best of both worlds: momentum-like direction smoothing and RMSprop-like adaptive rates.

questions.question: Match each learning rate problem to its consequence.
categories: Updates overshoot the minimum and loss may diverge
Convergence is extremely slow
Smooth convergence to a good solution
May oscillate around the minimum without settling in

correct_mapping.Learning rate too high: Updates overshoot the minimum and loss may diverge
correct_mapping.Good learning rate: Smooth convergence to a good solution
correct_mapping.No learning rate decay: May oscillate around the minimum without settling in

questions.explanation: A learning rate that is too high causes overshooting and instability. Too low means painfully slow progress. A well-chosen learning rate allows smooth, steady convergence. Without decaying the learning rate over time, the optimizer may keep bouncing around the minimum instead of settling precisely into it.

questions.question: Match each optimizer to what it uses to make update decisions.
categories: Current gradient only
Current gradient plus accumulated velocity from past gradients
Current gradient scaled by exponential moving average of squared gradients
First moment (gradient mean) and second moment (gradient variance) with bias correction

correct_mapping.Momentum: Current gradient plus accumulated velocity from past gradients
correct_mapping.RMSprop: Current gradient scaled by exponential moving average of squared gradients
correct_mapping.Adam: First moment (gradient mean) and second moment (gradient variance) with bias correction

questions.explanation: Each optimizer builds on the last. SGD uses just the raw gradient. Momentum adds a velocity from past gradients. RMSprop scales by recent squared gradients for per-parameter adaptation. Adam combines both the velocity (first moment) and the scaling (second moment), plus bias correction for accurate early estimates.

questions.question: Match each concept to its definition in the context of optimization.
categories: The direction and steepness of the loss surface at the current point
A hyperparameter that controls the step size
A measure of how wrong the model's predictions are
The change applied to weights after computing the gradient

correct_mapping.Gradient: The direction and steepness of the loss surface at the current point
correct_mapping.Learning rate: A hyperparameter that controls the step size
correct_mapping.Loss function: A measure of how wrong the model's predictions are
correct_mapping.Parameter update: The change applied to weights after computing the gradient

questions.explanation: The gradient tells us the slope of the loss surface, indicating which direction to move. The learning rate scales how far we move in that direction. The loss function is what we are trying to minimize. The parameter update is the actual adjustment made to the model weights each step.

questions.question: Match each momentum variant to its approach.
categories: Computes gradient at current position, then adds velocity
Takes a momentum step first, then computes gradient at the lookahead position

correct_mapping.Standard momentum: Computes gradient at current position, then adds velocity
correct_mapping.Nesterov momentum: Takes a momentum step first, then computes gradient at the lookahead position

questions.explanation: Standard momentum computes the gradient where the parameters currently are, then combines it with the accumulated velocity. Nesterov momentum does the opposite order: it first moves in the velocity direction to get a lookahead position, then computes the gradient there. This look-ahead gives a more informed gradient.

questions.question: Match each optimizer's weakness to the correct optimizer.
categories: Noisy updates because it only uses the current gradient
Learning rate monotonically decreases and can become too small
Can overshoot and oscillate around minima if momentum coefficient is too high

correct_mapping.Vanilla SGD: Noisy updates because it only uses the current gradient
correct_mapping.AdaGrad: Learning rate monotonically decreases and can become too small
correct_mapping.Momentum: Can overshoot and oscillate around minima if momentum coefficient is too high

questions.explanation: SGD is noisy because each step depends on just the current mini-batch gradient. AdaGrad's accumulated squared gradients only grow, eventually choking the learning rate to near zero. Momentum's accumulated velocity can carry the optimizer past the minimum if the momentum coefficient is set too aggressively.

questions.question: Match each Adam component to what it tracks.
categories: Mean of recent gradients (direction of updates)
Mean of recent squared gradients (magnitude scaling)
Compensates for zero-initialization in early training steps

correct_mapping.First moment estimate: Mean of recent gradients (direction of updates)
correct_mapping.Second moment estimate: Mean of recent squared gradients (magnitude scaling)
correct_mapping.Bias correction: Compensates for zero-initialization in early training steps

questions.explanation: Adam's first moment is an exponential moving average of the gradients, tracking the overall update direction like momentum. The second moment is an exponential moving average of squared gradients, used to scale each parameter's learning rate like RMSprop. Bias correction adjusts both estimates to be accurate despite starting from zero.

questions.question: Match the relationship between AdaGrad and RMSprop.
categories: Sums all past squared gradients (grows forever)
Uses exponential moving average of squared gradients (forgets old ones)
Learning rate shrinks to near zero
Learning rate stays in a useful range

correct_mapping.AdaGrad's squared gradient tracking: Sums all past squared gradients (grows forever)
correct_mapping.RMSprop's squared gradient tracking: Uses exponential moving average of squared gradients (forgets old ones)
correct_mapping.AdaGrad over long training: Learning rate shrinks to near zero
correct_mapping.RMSprop over long training: Learning rate stays in a useful range

questions.explanation: AdaGrad keeps a running total of squared gradients that can only increase, causing the effective learning rate to shrink continuously. RMSprop solves this by using an exponential moving average that gradually forgets older gradient information. This simple change keeps the learning rate from collapsing, making RMSprop viable for longer training runs.

questions.question: Match each benefit to the optimizer that provides it.
items: Helps escape local minima via accumulated velocity
Adapts step size per parameter based on gradient history
Provides both direction smoothing and per-parameter adaptation
categories: Momentum
RMSprop / AdaGrad
Adam
Vanilla SGD
questions.explanation: Momentum's velocity can carry the optimizer through shallow local minima. Adaptive methods like RMSprop and AdaGrad tailor the learning rate to each parameter. Adam combines both advantages. Vanilla SGD is the simplest and cheapest, requiring no extra memory for moment estimates.

questions.question: Match each optimizer property to whether it applies to SGD or Adam.
items: Uses a single global learning rate for all parameters
Adapts the learning rate independently for each parameter
Updates are often noisy and can zig-zag
Includes bias correction for moment estimates
categories: SGD
Adam
SGD
Adam
questions.explanation: SGD uses one learning rate for all parameters and its updates rely solely on the current gradient, making them noisy. Adam gives each parameter its own effective learning rate via the second moment estimate and includes bias correction to handle the zero-initialization of its moment estimates.
